question: [File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified] Can an unprivileged maker, under the precondition that they have ratified a Merkle root via SetterRatifier containing an offer with offer.callback set to a victim address that has approved Midnight for loanToken, call take() with that offer, triggering IBuyCallback(offer.callback).onBuy() on the victim and then safeTransferFrom(loanToken, offer.callback, address(this), buyerAssets - sellerAssets) and safeTransferFrom(loanToken, offer.callback, receiver, sellerAssets), violating the invariant that user funds must not be moved without their explicit authorization as payer, causing scoped impact: direct loss of loanToken from victim address? Proof idea: deploy victim contract with Midnight approval, maker ratifies root with offer.callback=victim, taker fills offer, assert victim balance decreases by buyerAssets.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / payer resolution line 422] Can an unprivileged taker, under the precondition that offer.buy == false and takerCallback is set to a third-party address that has approved Midnight and returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS from onBuy, call take() such that payer = takerCallback (not msg.sender), causing safeTransferFrom(loanToken, takerCallback, address(this), buyerAssets - sellerAssets) and safeTransferFrom(loanToken, takerCallback, receiver, sellerAssets) to pull tokens from the third party, violating the invariant that the explicit payer (msg.sender) cannot be bypassed, causing scoped impact: direct loss of loanToken from the takerCallback address? Proof idea: deploy a callback contract owned by victim with Midnight approval, taker sets takerCallback=victim_callback, assert victim_callback balance decreases.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified / take()] Can an authorized operator of a maker, under the precondition that the maker has authorized the operator via isAuthorized[maker][operator]=true, call setIsRootRatified(maker, maliciousRoot, true) to ratify a Merkle root containing offers with offer.callback set to a victim address, then call take() with those offers to drain the victim's loanToken balance, violating the invariant that gates and ratifiers must not be bypassable, causing scoped impact: unauthorized seizure of victim funds via coarse authorization abuse? Proof idea: maker authorizes operator, operator ratifies malicious root, operator calls take() with offer.callback=victim, assert victim loses tokens.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: repay() line 511] Can an unprivileged authorized operator, under the precondition that they are authorized for onBehalf and set callback to a victim address that has approved Midnight and returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS from onRepay, call repay(market, units, onBehalf, callback=victim, data) such that payer = victim, causing safeTransferFrom(loanToken, victim, address(this), units) to pull repayment tokens from the victim instead of msg.sender, violating the invariant that the explicit payer must be the caller or a consenting callback, causing scoped impact: victim pays for another user's debt repayment? Proof idea: authorized operator calls repay with callback=victim, assert victim balance decreases by units.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: liquidate() line 679] Can an unprivileged liquidator, under the precondition that a borrower is liquidatable and the liquidator sets callback to a victim address that has approved Midnight and returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS from onLiquidate, call liquidate() such that payer = victim, causing safeTransferFrom(loanToken, victim, address(this), repaidUnits) to pull repayment from the victim while the liquidator receives seized collateral, violating the invariant that user funds must not be stolen outside valid protocol rules, causing scoped impact: victim pays repaidUnits while liquidator profits from seized collateral? Proof idea: liquidator calls liquidate with callback=victim, assert victim loses loanToken and liquidator gains collateral.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() lines 420-456] Can a maker crafting a buy offer (offer.buy == true) with offer.callback set to address X, under the precondition that X has approved Midnight for loanToken and X's onBuy returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS, cause take() to pull both the settlement fee (buyerAssets - sellerAssets) AND the seller payment (sellerAssets) from X via two consecutive safeTransferFrom calls, violating the invariant that only the consenting buyer should pay buyerAssets, causing scoped impact: X loses buyerAssets = sellerAssets + fee without being the actual buyer? Proof idea: fuzz buyerAssets and sellerAssets, assert X.balanceBefore - X.balanceAfter == buyerAssets.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / SetterRatifier.isRatified] Can a taker, under the precondition that offer.buy == false and takerCallback is set to a contract that returns CALLBACK_SUCCESS without actually holding or approving any tokens, cause take() to revert only at the safeTransferFrom step (after state updates including consumed increment and position changes), violating the invariant that partial state updates must not persist on revert, causing scoped impact: accounting corruption where consumed[maker][group] is incremented but no tokens transferred? Proof idea: set takerCallback to a contract with no token balance, call take(), assert consumed state is rolled back on revert.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified] Can an unprivileged user, under the precondition that they have been authorized by maker A via isAuthorized[A][attacker]=true for any legitimate purpose, call setIsRootRatifier(A, root, true) to ratify a root containing offers where offer.callback is set to maker B (a different user who has approved Midnight), then fill those offers to drain maker B's tokens, violating the invariant that authorization must only allow intended account delegation, causing scoped impact: maker B's loanToken balance drained without their consent? Proof idea: maker A authorizes attacker, attacker ratifies root with offer.callback=makerB, attacker fills offer, assert makerB loses tokens.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() payer = buyerCallback line 422] Can a maker, under the precondition that offer.buy == true and offer.callback is set to a contract that implements onBuy to return CALLBACK_SUCCESS but does NOT transfer any tokens, cause take() to attempt safeTransferFrom from offer.callback after the callback returns, and if offer.callback has a pre-existing Midnight approval from a prior interaction, drain those pre-approved tokens, violating the invariant that token approvals to Midnight must only be consumed by the approving address's own actions, causing scoped impact: pre-approved tokens drained from offer.callback? Proof idea: deploy callback contract that approves Midnight in constructor, maker sets offer.callback to it, fill offer, assert tokens pulled from callback contract.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / multicall()] Can an attacker, under the precondition that they are authorized for a maker, use multicall to atomically: (1) call setIsRootRatified(maker, root, true) via SetterRatifier, (2) call take() with an offer from that root where offer.callback = victim, causing the victim's tokens to be pulled in the same transaction, violating the invariant that multicall must not bypass per-action invariants, causing scoped impact: atomic drain of victim's loanToken in a single multicall transaction? Proof idea: encode both calls in multicall bytes array, assert victim balance decreases atomically.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() line 422 / offer.buy == false] Can a taker, under the precondition that offer.buy == false (sell offer from maker), set takerCallback to address(maker) where maker has approved Midnight, causing payer = maker (via takerCallback), so the maker pays buyerAssets from their own balance even though the maker is the seller in this trade, violating the invariant that the seller must not be charged the buyer's payment, causing scoped impact: maker pays buyerAssets as both seller and forced payer? Proof idea: set takerCallback=maker.address, assert maker.loanToken balance decreases by buyerAssets.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / offer.receiverIfMakerIsSeller] Can a maker crafting a sell offer (offer.buy == false) with offer.callback set to victim and offer.receiverIfMakerIsSeller set to attacker, under the precondition that victim has approved Midnight, cause take() to pull sellerAssets from victim (as payer) and send them to attacker (as receiver), violating the invariant that the receiver of sellerAssets must be funded by the legitimate buyer, causing scoped impact: victim pays sellerAssets that flow directly to attacker? Proof idea: craft offer with callback=victim, receiverIfMakerIsSeller=attacker, fill offer, assert attacker gains sellerAssets and victim loses them.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: SetterRatifier.isRatified / HashLib.isLeaf] Can an attacker, under the precondition that they control a Merkle tree where an internal node hash (keccak256(left || right)) collides with a valid offer leaf hash, submit a proof that validates a different offer than the one the maker intended to ratify, violating the invariant that ratifier approval must bind the correct offer, causing scoped impact: offer with attacker-controlled offer.callback is ratified under a root the maker legitimately set? Proof idea: construct second-preimage collision between internal node and offer hash, submit as leaf proof, assert isLeaf returns true for unintended offer.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / no-op take with consumed offer] Can an attacker, under the precondition that an offer is fully consumed (consumed[maker][group] == offer.maxUnits) but has offer.callback set to victim, exploit the protocol comment 'Midnight can call the callback of offers through a no-op take, even if those offers have consumed==max' to trigger IBuyCallback(victim).onBuy() and then safeTransferFrom(loanToken, victim, ..., 0) with units=0, violating the invariant that fully consumed offers must not trigger new token pulls, causing scoped impact: victim's onBuy callback invoked without maker intent, potential reentrancy or state confusion? Proof idea: exhaust offer, call take with units=0, assert victim.onBuy is called and no tokens are pulled.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: repay() / authorization check] Can an unprivileged attacker, under the precondition that they are authorized for victim A (isAuthorized[A][attacker]=true) and victim B has approved Midnight, call repay(market, units, onBehalf=A, callback=B, data) to repay A's debt using B's tokens, violating the invariant that the repayment payer must be the caller or a consenting party, causing scoped impact: B's loanToken balance reduced to repay A's debt without B's consent? Proof idea: attacker authorized for A, calls repay with callback=B, assert B loses units of loanToken.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: liquidate() / payer confusion with collateral-first transfer] Can an unprivileged liquidator, under the precondition that a borrower is liquidatable and the liquidator sets callback=victim (victim has approved Midnight), call liquidate() such that: (1) seized collateral is sent to receiver=attacker first, (2) onLiquidate is called on victim, (3) repaidUnits are pulled from victim, violating the invariant that the liquidator must fund the repayment themselves, causing scoped impact: attacker receives seized collateral while victim pays repaidUnits? Proof idea: liquidator calls liquidate(callback=victim, receiver=attacker), assert attacker gains collateral and victim loses loanToken.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / payer determination before callback execution] Can a maker, under the precondition that offer.buy == true and offer.callback is set to a contract that during onBuy revokes its own Midnight approval (sets allowance to 0), cause take() to revert at the safeTransferFrom step after position state (buyerPos.credit, sellerPos.debt, consumed, totalUnits, claimableSettlementFee) has already been updated, violating the invariant that state updates must be atomic with token transfers, causing scoped impact: accounting corruption where credit/debt/consumed are updated but no tokens transferred? Proof idea: callback revokes approval in onBuy, assert take() reverts but check if state is rolled back.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / offer.buy == true, payer = offer.callback] Can a maker, under the precondition that offer.callback is set to a contract that implements onBuy to call Midnight.setIsAuthorized(victim, attacker, true) during the callback (reentrancy into a non-take function), then after the callback Midnight pulls tokens from offer.callback, violating the invariant that callbacks must not corrupt authorization state, causing scoped impact: attacker gains authorization over victim during take callback execution? Proof idea: callback calls setIsAuthorized in onBuy, assert isAuthorized[victim][attacker] is true after take.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified / offer.callback in hashOffer] Can a maker, under the precondition that they ratify a Merkle root containing an offer with offer.callback=victimContract, later claim they did not intend to designate victimContract as payer because the callback field was not clearly understood as a payer designation, and an attacker exploits this by filling the offer to drain victimContract, violating the invariant that ratifier approval must bind the correct maker intent including payer designation, causing scoped impact: victim contract's tokens drained via maker's ratified but misunderstood offer? Proof idea: verify hashOffer includes offer.callback, confirm maker's root ratification commits to callback-as-payer, assert victim loses tokens.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / takerCallback as payer when offer.buy == false] Can a taker, under the precondition that offer.buy == false and takerCallback is set to a contract that implements onBuy to return CALLBACK_SUCCESS and has a pre-existing loanToken approval to Midnight from a flashLoan interaction, cause take() to pull buyerAssets from takerCallback without takerCallback explicitly consenting to pay for this specific take, violating the invariant that pre-existing approvals must not be consumed by unrelated operations, causing scoped impact: takerCallback's pre-approved tokens consumed for a take it did not initiate? Proof idea: takerCallback approves Midnight during flashLoan, attacker uses it as takerCallback in subsequent take, assert approval consumed.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / payer = buyerCallback, receiver = offer.receiverIfMakerIsSeller] Can a maker crafting a sell offer (offer.buy == false) with offer.callback = address(0) and takerCallback set by taker to victimAddress, under the precondition that victim has approved Midnight, cause payer = victimAddress and receiver = offer.receiverIfMakerIsSeller (attacker-controlled), so victim pays sellerAssets that go to attacker's receiver address, violating the invariant that the taker (buyer) must fund the trade, causing scoped impact: victim funds flow to maker-controlled receiver? Proof idea: taker sets takerCallback=victim, maker sets receiverIfMakerIsSeller=attacker, assert attacker receives sellerAssets funded by victim.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified / isAuthorized coarse-grained] Can an attacker, under the precondition that maker has authorized attacker for a specific purpose (e.g., to call repay on their behalf), exploit the coarse-grained authorization to call SetterRatifier.setIsRootRatified(maker, maliciousRoot, true) where maliciousRoot contains offers with offer.callback=victim, then fill those offers, violating the invariant that authorized addresses must only perform intended actions, causing scoped impact: attacker uses legitimate authorization to ratify malicious offer trees and drain victim? Proof idea: maker authorizes attacker for repay, attacker calls setIsRootRatified, fills offer with callback=victim, assert victim loses tokens.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / sellerCallback called after safeTransferFrom] Can a seller (maker in sell offer), under the precondition that offer.callback is set to a contract that during onSell reenters take() with a new offer where offer.callback=victim, cause a nested take to pull tokens from victim while the outer take's state is partially committed, violating the invariant that multicall/callback/reentrancy must not break invariants that hold for single calls, causing scoped impact: victim's tokens drained via nested callback reentrancy? Proof idea: sellerCallback reenters take with offer.callback=victim, assert victim loses tokens in nested call.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: take() / payer confusion with fee-on-transfer loanToken] Can a maker, under the precondition that loanToken charges a fee on transfer and offer.callback is set to victim, cause take() to pull buyerAssets from victim but Midnight receives only buyerAssets * (1 - feeRate), while accounting records buyerAssets - sellerAssets as claimableSettlementFee and sellerAssets as transferred to receiver, violating the invariant that ERC20 transfer deltas must match accounting deltas, causing scoped impact: protocol insolvency where claimableSettlementFee exceeds actual received tokens? Proof idea: deploy fee-on-transfer token, set offer.callback=victim, fill offer, assert Midnight balance < expected accounting.

[File: 'src/ratifiers/SetterRatifier.sol -> Scope: High Callback payer confusion where tokens are pulled from the wrong party or an explicit payer can be bypassed'] [Function: repay() / callback payer with onBehalf authorization] Can an attacker, under the precondition that they are authorized for borrower B (isAuthorized[B][attacker]=true) and victim V has approved Midnight, call repay(market, B.debt, onBehalf=B, callback=V, data) to fully repay B's debt using V's tokens, then call withdrawCollateral on behalf of B to extract B's collateral to attacker's address, violating the invariant that user funds must not be stolen, causing scoped impact: V's tokens used to free B's collateral which attacker then withdraws? Proof idea: attacker repays B's debt with V's tokens, withdraws B's collateral, assert V loses loanToken and attacker gains collateral.
